focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

Blue Hour after close smelled of boiled lint, cautery gel, and the sweet old leak from the steam line Jo kept meaning to bully the landlord into fixing. The courier woman's rainwater still marked the threshold where she'd stood reading tomorrow's hours off the fresh sheet Mira had handed her. Mira had wiped the wetbench twice and still there was pink under one fingernail from a courier's borrowed ear. Tavi had the anomaly wafer under the reader lamp and was lining stripped screws along the lip of the table from smallest to largest, one of his worse habits when he was scared enough to look busy.

Lin sat on an upturned detergent bucket with the intake cards stacked on their knee, reading every label even after they already knew what the bottle or bin was for. The printed words gave them something solid to grip. Jo's old green cardigan swallowed their wrists. A thread had come loose at the cuff and Lin kept winding it around one finger without noticing.

The wafer was not much to look at. It was a translucent sliver the size of two postage stamps, archive-sealed, with one corner chipped when Mira had pried it loose in the records room with her thumbnail and a prayer she had not believed in. She had taped it inside the lining of her bag for two weeks and still checked every morning to make sure it was there. Tonight Tavi had fitted an adapter from a retired wash console to a clinic reader that still thought it was processing skin-tax invoices.

"If this shorts the board, that's on you," Mira said.

Tavi did not look up. "You're the one who stole corporate memory candy."

"You told me to steal proof."

"I told you to steal proof you could use without burning my only decent reader." He gave the cable a small twist and the lamp stopped flickering. "There. If it c...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...His next breath snagged, and his hands hung useless at his sides. Telling the truth had wrecked him in a different register than sex, and there was no taking it back.

She was still furious, still hot from him, still aware of the exact places his hands had been and the exact shape of the confession sitting between them like contaminated equipment nobody could throw out because they still needed it.

The worst part was the wanting, stubborn and intact. It stayed where he'd left it, alive in the same body that now had to carry what he'd told her.

Tavi shifted forward, saw her face, and stopped himself.

Mira slung her bag over one shoulder. "Tomorrow at eight," she said. "Mrs. Alvarado's knee."

Work was not pardon or dismissal. It was the only sentence she trusted not to lie.

"Yeah," he said. His voice caught and corrected. "Yeah. Eight."

She pushed through the curtain into the darkened laundromat. The machines were all dead now. Without them the room felt too large, the rows of glass doors like blank eyes. Jo was at the front gate with the padlock in one hand and her egg timer in the other, as if time itself might try to cheat her if she looked away.

"You all right?" Jo asked.

Mira almost gave the easy answer. Instead she said, "No."

Jo considered that, then nodded once and filed it away the way she filed unpaid tabs and useful gossip. "Good. Means you noticed something."

Outside, the canal wind had teeth in it. Mira stepped into it with Tavi's taste still in her mouth and the clean ugly fact of him lodged under her ribs: the man who had just held her like she was breakable had once helped CommonFrame calculate how much breakage a bargain body could carry and still turn a profit. By morning Jo would crank the gate back up, and the city would start knocking again.
